page: Vultr vbm-8c-128gb-amd is a bare metal server powered by an AMD EPYC 4345P processor built on the x86_64 architecture. This system features 16 dedicated physical cores and 16 vCPUs operating at a clock speed of 3.9 GHz, supported by 32 MB of L3 cache. The hardware configuration includes 123.0 GB of high-speed DDR5 memory running at 6 GHz, providing approximately 7.71 GB of RAM per core. For local storage, the server is equipped with a bundled 3890 GB NVMe SSD, and it includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU accelerators are present on this instance.

Benchmark evaluations indicate top-tier single-core performance, with stress-ng single-core CPU metrics and single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression rates placing in the top 10%. Passmark memory scores also rank in the top tier. However, multi-core CPU performance, database operations, and static web serving throughput fall within the average range. A notable performance tradeoff is observed in large-block write memory bandwidth, which registers in the bottom 25% at 21.85 GB/sec, contrasting with the average read bandwidth of 45.62 GB/sec.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, this bare metal server offers a dense resource profile, combining high-capacity DDR5 memory and substantial local NVMe storage without hypervisor overhead. This makes it a strong candidate for workloads requiring dedicated hardware resources and rapid local storage access. Recommended workloads include database hosting, memory-intensive applications, static web serving, and single-threaded processing tasks that benefit from its high single-core performance.
description: Vultr vbm-8c-128gb-amd is a bare metal server featuring an AMD EPYC 4345P processor with 16 dedicated physical cores and 16 vCPUs running at 3.9 GHz. It is equipped with 123.0 GB of DDR5 RAM operating at 6 GHz and a bundled 3890 GB NVMe SSD, providing a high-capacity storage and memory profile without GPU acceleration. Performance benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU execution and memory scores, alongside average multi-core and database performance. A key tradeoff is its lower large-block write memory bandwidth, which falls into the bottom 25%. Given its dedicated hardware architecture and balanced resource density, this server is well-suited for database hosting, memory-intensive applications, and static web serving workloads requiring high-speed local storage.
